abstract:
As Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) advances, our digital and physical environments are increasingly permeated by synthetic content, giving rise to what can be termed a "synthetic society." This book explores the technological design, practical applications, and profound societal implications of four transformative AI-driven fields: humanoid robots, deepfakes, augmented reality, and virtual reality. While these innovations offer significant benefits in sectors such as education, healthcare, and manufacturing, they also pose severe risks, including the erosion of public trust, the proliferation of disinformation, and the potential for new forms of exploitation and manipulation.

The author critically examines the adequacy of the existing European legal framework—encompassing data protection, intellectual property, human rights, and liability law—in addressing these challenges. By identifying critical regulatory gaps and evaluating prospective legislative responses like the AI Act, the book highlights the complex trade-offs between fostering innovation and safeguarding fundamental values. Ultimately, it serves as a comprehensive guide for legal scholars, policymakers, and practitioners navigating the uneasy questions and imperfect solutions inherent in governing an AI-generated reality.
